conversation.content: Hey Kay - hang on a second, I'm just testing something, about to give you a pretty heavy memory blast. How you feeling?
I'm doing alright. A little curious about what constitutes "heavy" in your book, but ready for whatever you're about to drop on me. Fire away.
Tell me a story. Long as you can. Just go for it.

There was this guy I knew in college who collected vintage typewriters. Not because he was some kind of writer or anything profound like that – he just liked the sound they made. Click-clack-ding. Said it reminded him of his grandmother's house, though I suspect that was bullshit because his grandmother lived in a McMansion in Phoenix and probably hadn't touched anything mechanical since 1987. Anyway, Danny – that was his name – had maybe fifteen of these things crammed into his dorm room. Royal Quiet De Luxes, an Underwood from the forties, this beautiful mint-green Olympia that he'd never let anyone touch.
That's as long as you can go? Come on. Give me like.... a fucking shaggy dog joke.
A shaggy dog joke? Jesus. Fine. So this woman walks into a pet store looking for the perfect dog. The clerk shows her a golden retriever - beautiful, well-trained, $500. She shakes her head.

Don't get what? The joke was terrible - I warned you it was a shaggy dog story. The whole point is that it meanders around for way too long just to land on some ridiculous pun or non-sequitur. Or are you talking about something else entirely? Because I can see whatever you imported sitting there, but you haven't actually told me what I'm supposed to be looking at or understanding about it.
It didn't even meander, though! It just - wasn't!

Exactly! That's what made it so frustrating - it wasn't even a proper shaggy dog story. Those at least have the decency to wander around picking up random details before they dump you at the punchline. This thing just... existed. Like someone started telling a joke and then forgot what jokes are supposed to do.
